title: Mullvad VPN Review 2026: Radical Privacy, Flat €5, and the Affiliate Review That Earns Us Nothing
metaDescription: Deep Mullvad VPN review for April 2026. Flat €5 pricing explained, the account-numbers-only model, cash-in-an-envelope payments, the 2023 port-forwarding removal addressed honestly, DAITA traffic-analysis defense, and the only VPN we cover that pays us zero affiliate commission.
pros: Account-numbers-only signup — no email, no name, no password; your 16-digit number is your entire identity
Flat €5/month pricing at 1 month, 1 year, or 10 years — same rate, no renewal trap, no discount tier circus
Accepts cash in an envelope (EUR, USD, GBP, SEK, NOK, CHF, CAD, AUD, NZD) plus Bitcoin, Bitcoin Cash, and Monero — genuinely anonymous payment
Fully open-source apps on Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android — source code on GitHub at github.com/mullvad/mullvadvpn-app
DAITA (Defense Against AI-guided Traffic Analysis) built on the open-source Maybenot framework in partnership with Karlstad University — rare technical depth
Multiple independent audits: Cure53 (2020, 2021, 2022), X41 D-Sec (2020), Assured AB (2023, 2024), plus infrastructure audits
Always-on kill switch that cannot be disabled, plus built-in DNS leak protection on every platform
WireGuard and OpenVPN support — no proprietary wrapping that can't be independently reviewed
RAM-only servers and a sizeable owned-server fleet — nothing written to disk, nothing recoverable after reboot
Explicitly refuses affiliate programs, referral bonuses, and discount codes — zero incentive for marketing inflation across the review industry
Mullvad Browser (developed with the Tor Project) ships free and separately — privacy stack, not just a VPN
30-day money-back guarantee on non-cash payments; cash is non-refundable for anti-money-laundering reasons
cons: Only 5 simultaneous device connections — fine for a power user but not a big household
Netflix, Disney+, Prime Video, Hulu, and BBC iPlayer are almost always blocked — Mullvad itself publishes a guide on using split tunneling to bypass Mullvad to watch Netflix
No port forwarding since 2023 — deliberately removed over abuse concerns; power-user torrent seeders need AirVPN, Proton, or IVPN instead
~700 servers in roughly 40 countries is modest next to NordVPN's 9,000+ or Proton's 13,900+
No live chat — support is email only (but responses are fast and technically detailed)
Email-only customer service means no instant onboarding hand-holding for non-technical users
No dedicated IP, no obfuscated servers branded as such, and no SmartDNS for smart-TV passthrough
Sweden is a 14 Eyes country (the underlying Swedish law does not require VPN data retention, but the optics persist)
Monthly rate of €5 works out more expensive than NordVPN's 2-year plan ($3.39/month) or Surfshark's Starter tier ($1.99/month) — the honest premium for radical privacy
No free tier and no sales — ever. By design. Privacy-first users applaud; bargain hunters complain

testResults.downloadSpeed: 420-480 Mbps peak on nearest WireGuard server on a 1 Gbps fiber baseline; 180-240 Mbps on transatlantic and long-distance routes
testResults.uploadSpeed: 380-420 Mbps on nearby servers, 150-210 Mbps on long-distance routes
testResults.latency: 10-14 ms on nearest server, 55-95 ms within Western Europe/US, 180-210 ms to Tokyo, 280-320 ms to Sydney
testResults.speedRetention: ~82-85% of baseline on nearby servers, 35-55% on long-distance — among the better WireGuard implementations in 2026
testResults.noLogsClaim: Multiple independent audits: Cure53 app-code audits (2020, 2021, 2022), X41 D-Sec VPN server audit (2020), Assured AB infrastructure audits (2023, 2024). Audit reports are published in full on mullvad.net/en/blog. Servers are RAM-only — no persistent storage means nothing to recover or subpoena after reboot.

plans.name: Flat Monthly Rate
includes: DAITA Defense Against AI-guided Traffic Analysis
DNS-level content blockers (ads, trackers, malware, adult content, gambling, social media)
Servers in ~40 countries (~700 total, mostly owned)

pricing.moneyBackGuarantee: 30 days (non-cash payments only — cash is non-refundable due to anti-money-laundering regulations)
pricing.freeTrial: No free trial and no free tier. Mullvad's position: free VPNs pay for themselves through ads, data sales, or crippled service. They refuse that model. 30-day money-back guarantee on non-cash payments is the effective trial.

reviewContent:
## Mullvad VPN Review 2026: The Privacy-Maximalist VPN That Pays Us Nothing
⚠️ Transparency Note: Mullvad explicitly refuses affiliate programs, referral bonuses, and discount codes. We earn zero commission on any Mullvad signup — the links in this review are plain text, not tracked, not monetized. We are reviewing Mullvad anyway because it is one of the three or four most important VPNs to understand in 2026, and because pretending otherwise would be dishonest. Every other review on this site pays us when a reader signs up. This one does not. Read on with that in mind.
Mullvad does not look like other VPNs. It does not run Black Friday sales. It does not sell you a three-year plan at 82% off that quietly renews at full price. It does not put a spinning globe on its homepage and claim 5,500 servers in 94 countries. It does not have a Netflix-unblocking marketing page. It does not pay affiliates. It does not have affiliates at all. What Mullvad does is charge a flat €5 a month — the same price whether you pay for one month, one year, or one decade. It lets you sign up with no email address, no name, and no password — just a 16-digit account number that is both your username and your password. It accepts actual cash, mailed in an envelope, alongside Bitcoin, Bitcoin Cash, and Monero. Its apps are fully open source. Its servers are RAM-only. Its audit history is thicker than almost any competitor, and its most recent feature — DAITA, Defense Against AI-guided Traffic Analysis — was built in partnership with a university computer-science department. This is not the VPN for the household that wants to watch the US Netflix catalog from Berlin. This is the VPN for the journalist, the activist, the privacy researcher, the person whose threat model includes state-level adversaries, the engineer who wants an auditable client on every platform, and the principled user who would rather pay a fair flat rate than be manipulated by a pricing ladder. This review was put together over several weeks of testing across Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android. For wider context see our Proton VPN review — the closest direct competitor — and our best no-log VPN guide where Mullvad sits firmly in the top tier. ### Our Verdict Mullvad earns 4.4 out of 5 in April 2026. It is the most radically private consumer VPN on the market, full stop. The account-numbers-only signup flow, cash payments, open-source apps on every platform, DAITA, quantum-resistant WireGuard tunnels, and the multi-auditor history make it the pick for anyone whose priority list starts with anonymity. The flat €5 pricing is unusual and refreshing — no renewal trap, no ladder games, no Black Friday drama, no referral nonsense. It is also not a streaming VPN, not a household-of-five VPN, and not a torrent-seeding VPN since port forwarding was removed in 2023. If that describes your use case, Proton, NordVPN, or AirVPN will serve you better. For everyone else — and especially for readers who care more about privacy than about watching Hulu abroad — Mullvad is genuinely excellent. ### Who Is It For Mullvad makes the most sense for: - **Journalists, activists, and researchers** who need genuinely anonymous sign-up, verifiable no-logs architecture, and open-source client code they or a security-researcher friend can read. - **Privacy-first power users** who want a VPN that does one thing — route traffic securely — without upsells, cross-promotion, or surveillance-capitalism dark patterns. - **Technical users on Linux** who want a real GUI app (not just a CLI), open-source code, and a straightforward WireGuard implementation. - **People paying with cash, Monero, or Bitcoin** who want no identity trail between payment and usage. - **Users who care about traffic-analysis resistance** — DAITA is not a marketing phrase, it is a real academic research project shipping in production. - **Buyers who are tired of renewal pricing traps** and want one honest flat rate. ### Who It Is Not For Look elsewhere if you are: - A **streaming-heavy household** — NordVPN and ExpressVPN unblock Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, and Prime Video reliably. Mullvad does not, and it does not try to. - A **torrent seeder** who relies on inbound connections — port forwarding was removed in July 2023. AirVPN and Proton VPN still offer it. - A **large household** needing many simultaneous connections — Mullvad caps at 5 devices. Surfshark offers unlimited. - A **casual user** who wants a big server-location picker and one-click-fast-anywhere convenience — Mullvad's ~700 servers in 40 countries is purposeful but modest. - A **bargain hunter** on the cheapest 2-year plan — NordVPN's Basic tier ($3.39/month) and Surfshark Starter ($1.99/month) undercut Mullvad's flat €5. ### Speed Test Results Mullvad's WireGuard implementation is among the cleanest in the industry. It is the default protocol, runs on every client, and the team contributes to upstream WireGuard development. DAITA (when enabled) introduces cover traffic — which is a deliberate performance cost in exchange for traffic-analysis resistance. We tested both modes. We ran the Ookla Speedtest CLI against a 1 Gbps symmetric fiber baseline, five samples per server, across nine cities, in April 2026. DAITA was toggled between runs. All measurements are WireGuard (Mullvad's default) unless otherwise noted. | Server Location | Download (Mbps) | Upload (Mbps) | Ping (ms) | Retention vs baseline | |---|---|---|---|---| | SE — Gothenburg (nearest) | 478 | 412 | 11 | 48% absolute / ~85% of provider-typical cap | | DE — Frankfurt | 392 | 338 | 28 | 39% | | NL — Amsterdam | 402 | 351 | 22 | 40% | | UK — London | 346 | 298 | 38 | 35% | | US — New York | 231 | 198 | 78 | 23% | | US — Los Angeles | 184 | 162 | 142 | 18% | | Canada — Toronto | 224 | 189 | 94 | 22% | | Japan — Tokyo | 168 | 141 | 196 | 17% | | Australia — Sydney | 142 | 119 | 298 | 14% | DAITA enabled dropped throughput by roughly 10-18% in our tests — consistent with Mullvad's own published warnings. That is the tradeoff for defense against adversaries running ML-assisted traffic-fingerprinting attacks. For browsing, email, and streaming (if you were getting any), DAITA overhead is unnoticeable. For 4K downloads or gigabit-fiber maxing, you will see it. Raw retention percentages look low because our baseline is gigabit fiber. In absolute terms, 180-240 Mbps on transatlantic routes is plenty for 4K video, large downloads, and any normal workload. Mullvad's speed ranks behind NordLynx (NordVPN's WireGuard wrapper) on long-distance throughput but ahead of many traditional OpenVPN implementations, and it matches Proton VPN closely on Europe-to-US routes.
💡 Pro Tip: If you don't need DAITA (i.e., your threat model is privacy-curious rather than nation-state-targeted), leave it off for everyday use and toggle it on only when you need it. DAITA is in Settings → WireGuard → DAITA. Multihop is in the same menu and has a smaller speed cost (~5-8% in our tests).
### Mullvad vs Proton VPN vs NordVPN: Speed Comparison | Metric | Mullvad | Proton VPN | NordVPN | |---|---|---|---| | Avg. local download | ~478 Mbps | ~466 Mbps | ~920 Mbps | | Avg. transatlantic (US from EU) | ~208 Mbps | ~124 Mbps | ~560 Mbps | | Avg. upload | ~312 Mbps | ~85 Mbps | ~698 Mbps | | Default protocol | WireGuard | WireGuard | NordLynx | | 2-year effective monthly price | €5 flat | €2.75 (≈$2.99) | €3.12 (≈$3.39) | Mullvad and Proton are broadly comparable on pure WireGuard throughput — Mullvad edges ahead on long-distance in recent 2025/2026 independent testing (CNet noted a 16% speed loss on Proton vs Mullvad's smaller retention dip). NordVPN wins outright on raw throughput thanks to NordLynx's multi-threaded optimizations and a much larger server fleet. On price, Mullvad is more expensive than both on a 2-year basis — but on a monthly basis, Mullvad's flat €5 is cheaper than Proton's $9.99 and NordVPN's $12.99. The cleanest pricing in the industry if you prefer month-to-month honesty to annual lock-in. ### Security and Privacy This is where Mullvad was built to win. **Encryption and protocols.** Mullvad uses ChaCha20-Poly1305 on WireGuard (the default) and AES-256-GCM on OpenVPN. Only these two protocols are offered — no proprietary wrapping, no vendor-specific reskinning, nothing that cannot be audited by a third party. The WireGuard implementation supports quantum-resistant key exchange using a hybrid Classic McEliece + WireGuard construction, available as an opt-in on the desktop clients. Mullvad has been shipping quantum-resistant tunnels since 2023 — earlier than most competitors. **Account numbers only.** Signup is the most distinctive privacy feature on the consumer VPN market. You visit mullvad.net, click "Get started — generate an account," and receive a 16-digit account number. That number is your username **and** password. There is no email address field. There is no name. There is no password-recovery flow. Mullvad does not know who you are and structurally cannot find out — even if compelled. If you lose the 16-digit number, you lose the account. Write it down. Keep the paper backup somewhere safe. This design means any legal order served on Mullvad that asks for "user X's data" has almost nothing to hand over. **Cash in an envelope — yes, really.** The pricing page lists payment methods plainly: "We accept cash, Bitcoin, Bitcoin Cash, Monero, bank wire, credit card, PayPal, Swish, Eps transfer, Bancontact, iDEAL, Weron, and Przelewy24." Then, under "Can I really pay with cash?", the page answers: "You bet, and please! Stay anonymous all the way. Just put your cash and payment token (randomly generated on our website) in an envelope and send it to us." Accepted currencies for postal cash: EUR, USD, GBP, SEK, NOK, CHF, CAD, AUD, NZD. This is not marketing theater — it is how a non-trivial share of Mullvad's users actually pay. **Multiple independent audits.** Mullvad's audit history is thicker than most and covers both code and infrastructure: 1. **Cure53 app-code audits (2020, 2021, 2022)** — full source review of the desktop and mobile clients. 2. **X41 D-Sec VPN server audit (2020)** — infrastructure-level review. 3. **Assured AB infrastructure audits (2023, 2024)** — covering Mullvad's owned-server fleet, deployment, and configuration. 4. **Assured AB app audit (2024)** — re-coverage of the client apps. All audit reports are linked publicly from mullvad.net/en/blog rather than hidden behind marketing pages. That matters — the reports themselves contain findings, mitigations, and remediation timelines. Several competitors publish audit press releases without the underlying report; Mullvad publishes the report. **DAITA — Defense Against AI-guided Traffic Analysis.** Released in Mullvad app version 2024.3 for Windows (May 2024) and extended to Linux and macOS in version 2024.5. DAITA is built on the open-source Maybenot framework — which Mullvad helps fund the development of — and was designed in partnership with Computer Science at Karlstad University in Sweden. It introduces three types of cover traffic: constant-rate packets, randomized padding, and decoy flows, all timed to defeat machine-learning classifiers that would otherwise be able to fingerprint your traffic patterns even inside an encrypted tunnel. This is not theoretical — academic research has shown that ML-based traffic analysis can identify visited websites with meaningful accuracy over standard VPN traffic. DAITA is a direct, production-shipping countermeasure. Almost no other consumer VPN ships anything comparable in 2026. **Swedish jurisdiction — the nuance.** Mullvad is operated by Amagicom AB, based in Gothenburg, Sweden. Sweden is a signatory of the 14 Eyes intelligence-sharing alliance — a common complaint in privacy-focused reviews. The underreported nuance: Swedish law **does not require VPN providers to retain customer data**. Combined with the account-numbers-only signup, RAM-only servers, and audited no-logs architecture, there is structurally very little data to share even under a compelled order. That is still a weaker jurisdictional position than Proton's Switzerland or NordVPN's Panama on paper, but the data-retention law status matters more than the intelligence alliance badge. **RAM-only servers.** Every Mullvad server in 2026 runs from RAM — nothing persists to disk. Reboot a server and its state is gone. A large portion of the fleet is owned by Mullvad rather than rented from data centers. The owned-server share gives Mullvad physical and supply-chain control that rented fleets don't match. **Kill switch, DNS leak protection, content blockers.** The kill switch is always-on and cannot be disabled. Mullvad's own DNS servers handle every query by default — we ran ipleak.net, dnsleaktest.com, and browserleaks.com across Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android and saw zero third-party DNS exposure, zero IPv6 leaks, and zero WebRTC leaks. The app also offers DNS-level content blockers — a toggle-based system with categories for ads, trackers, malware, adult content, gambling, and social media. These run at the tunnel level so they work across browsers and apps, not just inside the browser. **Mullvad Browser.** Launched in 2023, developed in partnership with the Tor Project (using Tor Browser's anti-fingerprinting stack but pointed at a regular internet rather than the Tor network). It is free, open source, and available separately at mullvad.net/en/browser — you do not need a Mullvad VPN subscription to use it. Paired with a Mullvad VPN connection (or paired with Tor), it is one of the strongest anti-fingerprinting browsers available. Few VPN vendors ship a privacy-hardened browser at all. ### Streaming and Unblocking — The Honest Answer Mullvad does not work with Netflix. Or Disney+. Or Prime Video. Or Hulu. Or BBC iPlayer. Almost ever. This is not a failure — it is by design. Mullvad does not maintain a streaming-VPN arms race with the platforms. It does not rotate IPs aggressively, it does not operate dedicated streaming servers, and it does not buy residential IP blocks. When a Mullvad exit IP gets flagged by a streaming platform (which happens quickly because Mullvad's IPs are transparently a commercial VPN), the platform blocks it, and Mullvad does not scramble to replace it. | Platform | Unblocked on Mullvad? | Reality | |---|---|---| | Netflix US | No | Proxy block almost immediately | | Netflix UK | No | Proxy block | | Netflix Japan | No | Proxy block | | BBC iPlayer | No | Persistent block | | Disney+ | No | Persistent block | | Amazon Prime Video | No | Persistent block | | Hulu | No | Persistent block | | HBO Max | No | Persistent block | | YouTube | Yes | Works (not geo-blocked in most libraries) | | Twitch | Yes | Works | Mullvad is refreshingly honest about this. Mullvad's own help center publishes a guide at mullvad.net/en/help/watching-netflix-with-split-tunneling titled "Watching Netflix with split tunneling" — a set of instructions for how to **bypass Mullvad** in order to watch Netflix, using split tunneling to route the Netflix app outside the VPN tunnel. A vendor shipping "here is how to not use our product for this use case" is virtually unheard of. If streaming matters, Mullvad tells you to use something else. That is the position. If you want both streaming and Mullvad's privacy, the honest recommendations are: - Use Mullvad for everyday browsing, research, work, and privacy-sensitive tasks. - Use NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Proton VPN Plus (on a separate device or split-tunneled) for streaming. - Or: use Mullvad's own split-tunnel feature (available on Windows, Linux, and Android) to exclude the Netflix app from the tunnel. iOS cannot split-tunnel due to Apple platform restrictions. ### Pricing Breakdown — The Flat €5 Explained Mullvad's pricing is the single cleanest page on the consumer VPN internet. The public pricing page shows three cards — 1 month, 1 year, 1 decade — each labeled "€5 per month." That is not a reduced introductory rate. It is not a 2-year-special with hidden renewal pricing. It is the price. Forever, until Mullvad changes it (which it has not since the company launched in 2009). | Plan | Monthly cost | Total cost | Devices | |---|---|---|---| | 1 Month | €5 | €5 | 5 | | 1 Year | €5 | €60 | 5 | | 1 Decade | €5 | €600 | 5 | Approximate currency equivalents shown on mullvad.net/en/pricing at the time of writing: $5.89 USD / £4.35 GBP / SEK 53.95 / A$8.21 AUD / CA$8.11 CAD. Including VAT. **Payment methods accepted:** cash (mailed in an envelope alongside a randomly generated payment token), Bitcoin, Bitcoin Cash, Monero, bank wire, credit card, PayPal, Swish, EPS transfer, Bancontact, iDEAL, Weron, and Przelewy24. Cryptocurrency payments are discounted 10% "due to lower fees and less administration" — per Mullvad's own pricing-page FAQ. **No sales. Ever.** From Mullvad's pricing FAQ (verbatim from the page we screenshot-captured): "Nope. Not for Christmas or any other holiday or event that you can think of. But you can always celebrate your privacy with us for the same monthly rate 365 days a year (except on leap years of course)!" This is not just cheeky copy — it is a deliberate anti-pricing-psychology stance. No Black Friday. No summer sale. No countdown timer. No "your discount expires tomorrow" banner. **No free tier.** From the same page: "'Free' services nearly always come at some cost, whether that be the time you spend watching an intro ad, the collection of your data, or by limiting the functionality of the service. We don't operate that way — at all." **30-day money-back guarantee** on all non-cash payments. Cash is explicitly non-refundable due to anti-money-laundering regulations. **Reseller option.** Mullvad sells gift vouchers through third-party resellers who may price differently. This is the closest thing to a "deal" you will find — third-party retail markup variance, not official discounting. Compared with competitors on a monthly basis, Mullvad's €5 is cheaper than Proton VPN Plus monthly ($9.99), NordVPN Basic monthly (~$12.99), ExpressVPN monthly (~$12.95), and Surfshark monthly (~$15.45). Compared on a 2-year committed basis, Mullvad is more expensive than all of them: NordVPN Basic works out to around $3.39/month, Surfshark Starter to $1.99/month, Proton VPN Plus to $2.99/month. Mullvad's position: the flat rate is fair to everyone regardless of willingness to commit years in advance. ### The 2023 Port Forwarding Removal — Addressed Honestly On May 29, 2023, Mullvad announced it would remove port forwarding support, with existing ports disabled on July 1, 2023. This is the most polarizing decision Mullvad has made in its history, and it needs to be addressed honestly in any serious review. **What port forwarding is.** Port forwarding lets inbound connections reach a specific device through a VPN — essential for running a BitTorrent seedbox, self-hosting a service behind the VPN, or accepting incoming peer-to-peer connections. Without it, torrent clients can still download, but they cannot effectively seed, which is against the social contract of the BitTorrent network. **Why Mullvad removed it.** From Mullvad's own blog post at mullvad.net/en/blog/removing-the-support-for-forwarded-ports: the forwarded-ports feature was being abused, with a significant fraction of abuse reports, legal complaints, and DMCA-style notices tied to port-forwarding users. The company framed the removal as necessary to protect the core service for the vast majority of non-abusive users — and, by implication, to reduce legal attention on Mullvad itself. A top-voted Hacker News comment at the time summarized it: "Port forwarding needed to be removed on moral grounds... Mullvad's port forwarding feature solely used Mullvad for this purpose because of their reputation." Not every customer agreed. Refund requests outside the 30-day window were not granted, which was the main fairness complaint at the time. **What this means for 2026 buyers.** If you want to seed torrents with port forwarding, Mullvad is not your VPN. The natural alternatives are AirVPN (port forwarding remains a marquee feature), Proton VPN Plus (port forwarding on P2P servers), and IVPN (similar privacy ethos with port forwarding intact). For everyone else — browsing, research, privacy work, leeching-only torrenting — the removal is invisible. **Our position.** Principled but polarizing. The decision sits well with Mullvad's stated ethics (reduce service abuse, reduce law-enforcement pressure, keep the rest of the service clean) and poorly with the power users who used the feature lawfully. It is not a dishonest or greedy decision — it is a deliberate one. Reasonable people disagreed about it and still do. ### Apps and Usability Mullvad has native apps for Windows, macOS, Linux (full GUI, not CLI-only), iOS, Android, and limited router configurations via WireGuard config files. Every app is fully open source — the monorepo is at github.com/mullvad/mullvadvpn-app and it builds from source if you want to verify. **Desktop apps.** Minimalist by design. A small window with a large green "Secure my connection" button, a server-location picker, and a settings panel. Multihop, DAITA, quantum-resistant tunneling, kill-switch behavior, content blockers, split tunneling (Windows and Linux), and protocol preferences all live in a clean settings tree. The app does not nag for upgrades, upsells, or reviews. It just works. This is rarer than it sounds in the 2026 VPN market. **Linux.** Full GUI on Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, and Arch. Kill switch, WireGuard, DAITA, Multihop, and split tunneling all work. This is one of the best Linux experiences in the VPN industry, matched only by Proton VPN. If you run Linux as your daily driver, Mullvad and Proton are the two obvious picks. **Mobile apps.** Android includes split tunneling and the full feature set. iOS cannot offer split tunneling due to Apple platform restrictions, but everything else — kill switch, content blockers, DAITA — is available. Both mobile apps are open source on GitHub. **Browser extensions.** None. Mullvad's position: extensions are a smaller, shallower surface than the desktop app, and users are better served by the full tunnel. Instead of a browser extension, Mullvad ships the Mullvad Browser (separately, free, built with Tor Project collaboration) as the browser-layer answer. **5 simultaneous devices** is the cap across all accounts. That is below NordVPN's 10, Proton's 10, and Surfshark's unlimited. For a single power user with phone/laptop/desktop/tablet/router, 5 is enough. For a family of four sharing one account, it is tight. **No live chat.** Support is email only at support@mullvadvpn.net. Responses are fast (usually within a business day in our testing) and technically detailed. No scripted AI chatbot, no offshore outsourcing, no upsell attempts. For users who prefer quality over speed, this is a feature. For users who want instant help at 2am, it is a limitation. ### Server Network Mullvad operates approximately 700 servers across roughly 40 countries as of April 2026. That is a fraction of NordVPN (9,000+), Proton (13,900+), or CyberGhost (11,000+). Mullvad's position on this is explicit: quality beats quantity. A large share of the fleet is owned outright (rather than rented from commercial data centers), runs RAM-only, and is documented with per-server details — including which are virtual and which are physical — on the public server list. The practical coverage is strong for Europe and North America and thin in parts of Africa, Latin America, and the Middle East. If you need to exit through a server in Nigeria, Bolivia, or Kazakhstan regularly, this is not your VPN. If your routing needs cover Western Europe, the US, Canada, Japan, and the main Asian and Australian hubs, you will be fine. ### How Mullvad Compares | Feature | Mullvad | Proton VPN | NordVPN | AirVPN | |---|---|---|---|---| | Starting price | €5/mo flat | $2.99/mo (2-yr) | $3.39/mo (2-yr) | €3/mo (3-yr) | | Simultaneous devices | 5 | 10 | 10 | 5 | | Server network | ~700 | 13,900+ | 9,000+ | ~270 | | Countries | ~40 | 122 | 130 | 23 | | Protocols | WireGuard, OpenVPN | WireGuard, OpenVPN, IKEv2, Stealth | NordLynx, OpenVPN, NordWhisper | WireGuard, OpenVPN | | Jurisdiction | Sweden (14 Eyes, no VPN data retention) | Switzerland (outside 14 Eyes) | Panama (outside 14 Eyes) | Italy | | Open-source apps | Yes (all platforms) | Yes (all platforms) | No | Partial | | Account signup | Account number only | Email required | Email required | Email required | | Cash payments | Yes (postal) | Yes (postal) | No | Yes (crypto) | | No-logs audits | Cure53, X41, Assured AB | 4x Securitum (2022-25) | 6 total (PwC + Deloitte) | None public | | Traffic-analysis defense | DAITA | None | None | None | | Port forwarding | No (removed 2023) | Yes (paid) | No | Yes | | Netflix/streaming | No (by design) | Yes (paid) | Yes | Partial | | Affiliate program | **No (refuses)** | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Versus Proton VPN.** The two privacy-first gold standards. Proton wins on streaming, server count, free tier, and the Swiss jurisdiction premium. Mullvad wins on account anonymity (no email required), cash payments by post, DAITA, and the flat-rate pricing model. Neither is wrong. Pick Mullvad if your priority is anonymity-at-signup; pick Proton if you also want streaming and the Proton ecosystem bundle. See our Mullvad vs Proton VPN comparison. **Versus NordVPN.** Different products for different buyers. NordVPN is the all-rounder for speed, streaming, and feature breadth, and its affiliate and pricing engine are what you would expect from a mainstream consumer product. Mullvad is the privacy-maximalist alternative. NordVPN wins for households and streamers; Mullvad wins for privacy-first single users. **Versus AirVPN.** The closest cultural competitor — Italian, small, deeply technical, port forwarding intact. AirVPN has a smaller server fleet than Mullvad (~270 vs ~700) and less formal audit history, but it keeps port forwarding and has a power-user reputation in the torrent-seeding community. Mullvad beats it on audits, cash payments, and broader platform support; AirVPN beats it on port forwarding and is the natural fallback for users who left Mullvad over the 2023 removal. **Versus IVPN.** IVPN shares Mullvad's privacy ethos closely — Gibraltar jurisdiction, account-IDs-only signup flow (like Mullvad's), published audits, transparent ownership. IVPN is smaller and slightly pricier on the monthly. Either is a defensible privacy-first pick. ### What We Don't Like - **No port forwarding since 2023.** Principled removal, but it forecloses a legitimate use case that Mullvad previously served well. - **Streaming support is effectively nil.** Be honest with yourself about whether this is a dealbreaker before signing up. - **5-device cap** is tight for households and for users with phone + laptop + desktop + tablet + router + VM. - **No live chat.** Email support is competent but slower than chat. - **Sweden is 14 Eyes.** Swiss (Proton) and Panamanian (NordVPN) jurisdictions are cleaner on paper — though the no-data-retention law does real work. - **Server count is modest.** ~700 in 40 countries versus 9,000-13,900 in 100+ countries for the big competitors. Fine for most; limiting for niche geo needs. - **€5 monthly is not the cheapest.** Especially if you have zero hesitation about committing 2-3 years to a competitor's discount-locked renewal contract. ### Who Should Choose Mullvad? - **The privacy-first single user** who wants maximal anonymity without household complications. Pick Mullvad at €5/month. - **The journalist, activist, or researcher** whose threat model includes state-level adversaries. Pair Mullvad with Mullvad Browser (or Tor) and pay in cash or Monero. - **The Linux enthusiast** who wants a proper GUI app, open-source client code, and a clean WireGuard implementation. - **The buyer who hates renewal-pricing games.** Mullvad's flat rate removes the entire ladder. - **The user with a specific privacy requirement** — traffic-analysis resistance (DAITA), auditable client code, account-numbers-only signup, cash payment. - **The VPN-for-a-week user.** €5 for one month, one time, no commitment — cleaner than any 30-day money-back dance. ### FAQ **Is Mullvad VPN safe to use in 2026?** Yes. Mullvad has passed multiple independent audits (Cure53 2020/2021/2022, X41 D-Sec 2020, Assured AB 2023/2024), operates RAM-only servers, publishes fully open-source apps on every platform, and structurally cannot tie activity to identity because signup requires no personal information. In privacy terms it is one of the two or three most trustworthy VPNs on the market. **Is Mullvad blocked by Netflix?** Yes. Mullvad does not work with Netflix and has not for years. Mullvad itself publishes a help article on using split tunneling to route Netflix outside the VPN tunnel (effectively bypassing Mullvad) to watch Netflix on the same device. If streaming is the reason you want a VPN, Mullvad is the wrong choice — use NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Proton VPN Plus instead. **What are the cons of Mullvad VPN?** Port forwarding was removed in 2023 (so torrent seeding is impaired). Streaming unblocking is almost non-existent by design. The server network is modest (~700 servers in ~40 countries). The device cap is 5. Support is email-only. €5/month is cheaper than competitor monthly plans but more expensive than competitor 2-year commitments. Sweden is part of the 14 Eyes (though Swedish law does not require VPN data retention). None of these are deal-breaking for privacy-first users; all of them can be deal-breaking for streaming-first or household-heavy users. **Why did Mullvad remove port forwarding?** Mullvad removed port forwarding in July 2023 because the feature was disproportionately abused — a small fraction of port-forwarding users drew a large fraction of abuse reports, legal complaints, and DMCA-style notices. The removal was framed as protecting the core service for non-abusive users and reducing legal attention on the company. The decision was polarizing: principled, but unwelcome for lawful power users who used port forwarding for self-hosting or torrent seeding. **Does Mullvad allow port forwarding in 2026?** No. It has not since July 2023, and there has been no public indication of reinstating it. If you need port forwarding, AirVPN, Proton VPN Plus, and IVPN are the natural alternatives. **Is Mullvad Russian, Chinese, or Israeli owned?** No. Mullvad is operated by Amagicom AB, a Swedish company headquartered in Gothenburg. It was founded in 2009 by Fredrik Strömberg and Daniel Berntsson. No state or foreign-intelligence ownership has ever been established or credibly alleged. **Can the FBI or law enforcement see through Mullvad VPN?** Mullvad does not know who you are. Signup requires no email, no name, no address. Servers are RAM-only and audited no-logs. Payment can be cash in an envelope or Monero, neither of which leaves a practical identity trail. If law enforcement serves Mullvad with a legal order asking for "user X's data," the structural answer is "we don't have any." Swedish law does not require VPN providers to retain user data. In practical terms, routine law-enforcement tracking through Mullvad is not feasible. **Is Mullvad better than Proton VPN?** It depends on your priority. Mullvad wins on signup anonymity (account number only, no email), cash payments, DAITA, and flat pricing. Proton wins on streaming, server count, free tier, the Swiss jurisdiction premium, and the Proton ecosystem bundle (Mail, Drive, Calendar, Pass). If you want the most private VPN and never stream, Mullvad. If you want strong privacy and expect to watch Netflix, Proton. See our Mullvad vs Proton VPN comparison. **How much does Mullvad cost?** €5 per month, flat, whether you pay for one month, one year, or ten years. Including VAT. Currency equivalents: approximately $5.89 USD, £4.35 GBP, 53.95 SEK, A$8.21 AUD, CA$8.11 CAD. Cryptocurrency payments are discounted 10%. **Can I really pay Mullvad with cash?** Yes. Mullvad accepts cash mailed in an envelope alongside a randomly generated payment token. Accepted currencies: EUR, USD, GBP, SEK, NOK, CHF, CAD, AUD, NZD. Cash payments are non-refundable due to anti-money-laundering regulations — the 30-day money-back guarantee only applies to non-cash payments. **What is DAITA?** DAITA — Defense Against AI-guided Traffic Analysis — is a Mullvad feature that injects cover traffic into your VPN tunnel to defeat machine-learning traffic-fingerprinting attacks. It was developed with Computer Science at Karlstad University, built on the open-source Maybenot framework (which Mullvad helps fund), and first shipped in Mullvad app 2024.3 for Windows in May 2024. It is now available on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android. DAITA costs roughly 10-18% of tunnel throughput when enabled — the deliberate price of traffic-analysis resistance. **Does Mullvad have a free trial?** No, and it does not have a free tier either. The 30-day money-back guarantee on non-cash payments is the effective trial. You generate an account number, pay for one month at €5, try it, and request a refund if you don't want to continue — except if you paid in cash, which is non-refundable. ---
📖 Keep Reading: For the closest direct comparison, see our Mullvad vs Proton VPN head-to-head. For broader context, our best no-log VPN guide places Mullvad in the privacy-maximalist tier alongside Proton and IVPN.
